note: Single initial used for personal names; most common referents in corpus are Anders and Anna
German Army Corps designation — appears as 'XX A.K.' and '20. A.K.' in address headers
Address — used in letter headers and when referring someone to a postal address
Single initial for personal names; Bodil and Barsballe are frequent referents
German: Battalion — variant spelling alongside Battl.; appears in unit addresses and tactical references
German: Battalion — alternate spelling of Batl.; used interchangeably in the corpus
Among / including — Danish discourse particle
Single initial for personal names
Approximately — used for distances, numbers, and time estimates
Abbreviated given name; both Christian and Christen are attested in the corpus (e.g., Chr. Bejer)
The / on the (date marker) — high frequency; used before dates and as definite article abbreviation
German: Division — appears in unit addresses such as '37. Div. XX A.K.' and 'Inf. Div.'
Various / miscellaneous — Danish discourse word
Doctor — used as honorific before a name (e.g., Dr. Schiffere); also appears for military physicians
That is / i.e. — Danish clarifying phrase
Single initial for personal names
Afternoon — used frequently in scheduling and daily routine descriptions
Possibly / if applicable — Danish modal adverb
For or From — low frequency; interpretation depends on syntactic context
February — month abbreviation used in dates
German: Sergeant (senior NCO rank) — alternate spelling of Feldw.; both forms appear in corpus
German: Sergeant (senior NCO rank) — the most senior non-commissioned officer rank; used frequently in narrative passages
Mrs. or Miss — Danish female honorific; Fru for married, Frøken for unmarried women
Single initial for personal names
German: Private First Class — the rank held by Peter Mærsk for much of his service; appears in address headers and narrative
Single initial for personal names
German: Captain — company commander rank; appears in address headers and narrative references
Mr. / Sir — German/Danish honorific used in formal address; appears on envelope addresses
Single initial for personal names; Iver is a frequent referent
Infantry — appears in unit designations such as 'Inf. Regt. 147' and 'Inf. Reg. 85'
Single initial for personal names; most common referent is Jørgen or surnames beginning with J (e.g., J. Jensen, J. Thøjsen)
January — month abbreviation used in dates
Single initial for personal names
Kilometer — used for distances, especially in descriptions of marches and troop movements
Kilometer — lowercase variant of Kilom.; same usage
O'clock — used extensively with train times and daily schedules (e.g., Kl. 9, Kl. 4)
O'clock — lowercase variant of Kl.; same usage
Company — military sub-unit; appears in unit addresses (e.g., '12. komp. Inf.-Regt. 147')
Company — lowercase variant of Komp.; same military unit usage
District / County — administrative division; appears in addresses as 'Kr. Hadersleben' (Haderslev district)
Single initial for personal names; Laurids is a frequent referent in the corpus
German: Military hospital / field hospital — appears in 'Res. Laz.' (reserve hospital) addresses
German: Lieutenant — junior officer rank; appears frequently in narrative as direct superior
Similar / and such like — Danish discourse particle used to extend lists (e.g., 'Skiver og lign.')
Single initial for personal names; one of the most frequent single-letter abbreviations in the corpus
With — Danish preposition; used in phrase-final position
Single initial for personal names
Number — used for package numbers, unit numbering (e.g., 'Pakke No. 22', 'Artilleriet No. 73')
Number — German/Danish variant of No.; used interchangeably for unit and item numbering
German: Officer — generic term for commissioned officer; used in 'Unteroffz.' compound and standalone
October — month abbreviation used in dates
East Prussia — appears in addresses and postmarks (e.g., 'Braunsberg Ostpr.')
Et cetera / and so on — Danish discourse particle
Single initial for personal names; very high frequency, most often refers to Peter Mærsk himself or Peter as a surname initial
Per or by way of — used in postal addresses ('pr. Roagger') and rates ('pr. Ltr.', 'pr. dag')
Prussia — appears in geographical references; also used as 'Per' in rates and addresses
Single initial for personal names
Regiment — short variant of Regt.; appears frequently in unit addresses and narrative
Regiment — primary abbreviation for the regimental unit designation (e.g., 'Inf.-Regt. 147')
Regiment — lowercase variant of Regt.
Reserve — used in unit designations such as 'Res. Lazaret', 'Res. Armeekorps', and 'I Res.'
Single initial for personal names
Schleswig — appears in postal addresses as 'Schl. Holstein' or 'Schl.Holst.' denoting the province
September — month abbreviation used in dates
Either a family name abbreviation (Laurids St. = Stenderup) or place prefix (St. = Sankt/Saint in French place names)
Single initial for personal names
German: Non-commissioned officer / Corporal — appears in compound with Offz. and standalone
Single initial for personal names
